> **Implementation consequence:** Annex B models intermediated operation through explicit `usesIntermediary` relationships. It does **not** rely on a standalone `isIntermediary` flag as the primary normative expression of that relationship.

**Class `LegalEntity` (`B.2.2`)**

| Attribute | Mult. | Type | RP-facing meaning |
|:----------|:-----:|:-----|:------------------|
| `legalPerson` | `[0..1]` | `LegalPerson` | Legal-person-specific attributes. Only one of `legalPerson` or `naturalPerson` may be present. |
| `naturalPerson` | `[0..1]` | `NaturalPerson` | Natural-person-specific attributes. Only one of `legalPerson` or `naturalPerson` may be present. |
| `identifier` | `[0..*]` | `Identifier` | Official identifiers anchored in authoritative records. |
| `postalAddress` | `[1..1]` | `string` | Postal address of the entity. |
| `country` | `[1..1]` | `string` | ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 country code, or `EU` for European-level providers. |
| `email` | `[0..*]` | `string` | One or more RFC 5322 email addresses. |
| `phone` | `[0..*]` | `string` | One or more international phone numbers, beginning with `+` and country code. |
| `infoURI` | `[0..*]` | `string` | General-purpose public information pages about the entity. |

> **Support vs information:** Annex B is explicit that `infoURI` complements, but does not replace, the dedicated support channels carried in `supportURI`.

**Class `LegalPerson` (`B.2.3`)**

| Attribute | Mult. | Type | RP-facing meaning |
|:----------|:-----:|:-----|:------------------|
| `legalName` | `[1..*]` | `string` | Official legal name(s) of the legal person as recorded in authoritative records. |
| `establishedBylaw` | `[0..*]` | `Law` | Legal basis by which the legal person is established. |

**Class `NaturalPerson` (`B.2.4`)**

| Attribute | Mult. | Type | RP-facing meaning |
|:----------|:-----:|:-----|:------------------|
| `givenName` | `[1..1]` | `string` | First name(s), including middle names where applicable. |
| `familyName` | `[1..1]` | `string` | Last name(s) or surnames. |
| `dateOfBirth` | `[0..1]` | `string` | Date of birth from official records. |
| `placeOfBirth` | `[0..1]` | `string` | Place of birth from official records. |

**Class `Identifier` (`B.2.5`)**

| Attribute | Mult. | Type | RP-facing meaning |
|:----------|:-----:|:-----|:------------------|
| `type` | `[1..1]` | `string` | URI-identified identifier scheme. ETSI TS 119 475 defines at least `EORI-No`, `LEI`, `EUID`, `VATIN`, `TIN`, and `Excise`; additional national/EU types may also exist. |
| `identifier` | `[1..1]` | `string` | Concrete identifier value for the `LegalEntity`. |

**Class `MultiLangString` (`B.2.6`)**

| Attribute | Mult. | Type | RP-facing meaning |
|:----------|:-----:|:-----|:------------------|
| `lang` | `[1..1]` | `string` | Two-letter Alpha-2 language code according to ISO 639. |
| `content` | `[1..1]` | `string` | Localized text value. |

> **Where it appears:** `MultiLangString` structures both `srvDescription` and `IntendedUse.purpose`, so multilingual display is a built-in part of the model rather than a presentation-layer afterthought.

**Class `IntendedUse` (`B.2.7`)**

| Attribute | Mult. | Type | RP-facing meaning |
|:----------|:-----:|:-----|:------------------|
| `purpose` | `[1..*]` | `MultiLangString` | One or more localized statements of the purpose of data processing. |
| `privacyPolicy` | `[1..*]` | `Policy` | Privacy-policy reference(s) associated with this intended use. |
| `createdAt` | `[1..1]` | `string` | Validity start date in `YYYY-MM-DD` format. |
| `revokedAt` | `[0..1]` | `string` | End date for the intended use if it is no longer valid. |
| `credential` | `[1..*]` | `Credential` | Requestable attestation/credential definitions for this intended use. |
| `intendedUseIdentifier` | `[1..1]` | `string` | Registrar-provided unique intended-use identifier. |

> **ETSI TS 119 475 note:** ETSI TS 119 475 Annex B describes `intendedUseIdentifier` as optional and reserved for future implementations even though the class table gives it multiplicity `[1..1]`. For RP analysis, the important point is that ETSI TS 119 475 expects an intended-use identifier slot in the model, especially where the register exposes one directly.

**Class `Policy` (`B.2.8`)**

| Attribute | Mult. | Type | RP-facing meaning |
|:----------|:-----:|:-----|:------------------|
| `type` | `[1..1]` | `string` | URI identifying the policy type. In WRP attribute context, ETSI TS 119 475 defines only the privacy-policy URI type. |
| `policyURI` | `[1..1]` | `string` | URL where the policy is published. |

**Class `Credential` (`B.2.9`)**

| Attribute | Mult. | Type | RP-facing meaning |
|:----------|:-----:|:-----|:------------------|
| `format` | `[1..1]` | `string` | Credential/attestation format. |
| `meta` | `[1..1]` | `string` | Format-specific metadata or properties used to identify the credential type. |
| `claim` | `[0..*]` | `Claim` | Claim set that can be requested or issued. If absent, the WRP is not declaring specific attributes at this level. |

**Class `Claim` (`B.2.10`)**

| Attribute | Mult. | Type | RP-facing meaning |
|:----------|:-----:|:-----|:------------------|
| `path` | `[1..1]` | `string` | Path pointer identifying the claim within the credential structure. |
| `values` | `[0..1]` | `string` | Optional expected-value constraint set for the claim. ETSI TS 119 475 describes this as an array of strings, integers, or booleans even though the type column is rendered as `string`. |

**Class `Law` (`B.2.11`)**

| Attribute | Mult. | Type | RP-facing meaning |
|:----------|:-----:|:-----|:------------------|
| `lang` | `[1..1]` | `string` | Two-letter Alpha-2 language code according to ISO 639. |
| `legalBasis` | `[1..1]` | `string` | Legal basis under which the legal person is established, or under which access to a specific claim is required or recommended. |

> **Scope note:** ETSI TS 119 475 says `Law` is only used in the context of `LegalPerson`, so it is not a general-purpose free-standing claim-justification object across the whole model.

Taken together, Annex B gives a more precise RP-centric picture than the earlier TS5/TS6 summary alone: it separates support from informational contact channels, treats multilingual service descriptions and purposes as structured data, models intended-use lifecycle explicitly, and carries intermediary/public-body/provider semantics in a way that later maps directly into WRPRC payloads.

#### 4.3 Registration Process Overview

Each Member State must publish one or more national registration policies (CIR 2025/848 Art. 4). These policies must include at minimum the following 7 content items (Art. 4(3)):

| Item | Art. 4(3) | Content |
|:-----|:----------|:--------|
| 1 | (a) | Identification and authentication procedures for RPs during registration |
| 2 | (b) | Required supporting documentation (identity, business registration, entitlements) |
| 3 | (c) | Authentic sources or official electronic records relied upon for data accuracy |
| 4 | (d) | Any other information or evidence required as part of the process |
| 5 | (e) | Automated means for RPs to register or update an existing registration (where applicable) |
| 6 | **(f)** | **Redress mechanism** available to RPs under MS law — i.e., what recourse an RP has if registration is rejected or suspended |
| 7 | (g) | Rules and procedures for verifying the RP's identity and other relevant information |

> **RP relevance**: Item (f) — the redress mechanism — is particularly important. If an RP's registration is rejected or suspended ([§4.3.2](#432-suspension-and-cancellation-of-registration-art-9)), the MS registration policy must specify how the RP can appeal or seek administrative review. RPs preparing for registration should consult their MS's published policy to understand the available remedies.

##### 4.3.1 Registration Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

```mermaid
---
config:
  themeVariables:
    noteBkgColor: "transparent"
    noteBorderColor: "transparent"
  sequence:
    messageAlign: left
    noteAlign: left
    actorMargin: 250
---
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant RP as 🏦 Relying Party
    participant REG as 🏛️ Registrar<br/>(Member State)
    participant ACA as 🔐 Access CA
    participant RCP as 📜 Registration<br/>Cert Provider

    rect rgba(148, 163, 184, 0.14)
    Note over RP,RCP: Phase 1: Application
    RP->>REG: Submit registration application
    Note right of RP: Includes: legal identity, contact info,<br/>intended attributes, purposes,<br/>intermediary status (if applicable),<br/>intended use cases
    REG->>REG: Validate application
    Note right of REG: Verify legal entity, check<br/>entitlements per national policy
    REG-->>RP: Registration confirmed
    Note right of RP: RP data published in national register
    Note right of RCP: ⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀
    end

    rect rgba(52, 152, 219, 0.14)
    Note over RP,RCP: Phase 2: Access Certificate Issuance
    RP->>ACA: Request WRPAC(s)
    Note right of RP: One per RP Instance
    ACA->>REG: Verify RP registration
    REG-->>ACA: Confirm registration
    ACA->>ACA: Issue X.509 certificate
    Note right of ACA: Contains: RP identity, MS,<br/>certificate policies
    ACA-->>RP: WRPAC issued
    ACA->>ACA: Log to Certificate Transparency
    Note right of RCP: ⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀
    end

    rect rgba(46, 204, 113, 0.14)
    Note over RP,RCP: Phase 3: Registration Certificate (Optional)
    RP->>RCP: Request WRPRC
    RCP->>REG: Retrieve registration data
    REG-->>RCP: Registration data
    RCP->>RCP: Issue WRPRC
    Note right of RCP: Embeds: entitlements,<br/>credentials, purpose,<br/>support and status info
    RCP-->>RP: WRPRC issued
    Note right of RCP: ⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀
    end
```

<details>
<summary><strong>1. Relying Party submits registration application to Registrar</strong></summary>

The Relying Party submits a registration application to the Registrar in its Member State. This submission formalizes its intent to request specific attributes (such as PID or SCA) from Wallet Users. The exact authenticated submission flow is still Member State-specific, and TS5 explicitly leaves full `POST /wrp` harmonization for further study, but the registration payload still follows the shared TS2/TS5/TS6 data model.

**Failure Path:** If the submitted data model fails schema validation, the Registrar returns a `400 Bad Request` and terminates the flow.

**Audit Telemetry:** The Relying Party triggers a `REGISTRATION_SUBMISSION_INITIATED` SIEM event.

```json
{
  "country": "DE",
  "identifier": [
    {
      "type": "http://data.europa.eu/eudi/id/EUID",
      "identifier": "5299001GCLKH6FPVJW75"
    }
  ],
  "legalPerson": {
    "legalName": ["Example Bank AG"]
  },
  "tradeName": "Example Bank",
  "supportURI": ["https://support.example-bank.de/eudi-wallet"],
  "srvDescription": [
    [
      {
        "lang": "en",
        "content": "Retail banking and digital onboarding"
      }
    ]
  ],
  "entitlements": [
    "https://uri.etsi.org/19475/Entitlement/Service_Provider"
  ],
  "supervisoryAuthority": {
    "name": "Der Bundesbeauftragte fuer den Datenschutz und die Informationsfreiheit",
    "country": "DE",
    "formURI": [
      "https://www.bfdi.bund.de/DE/Service/Kontakt/kontakt_node.html"
    ]
  },
  "intendedUse": [
    {
      "intendedUseIdentifier": "urn:eudi:wrp:de:bank-example:kyc",
      "purpose": [
        {
          "lang": "en",
          "content": "Customer onboarding and KYC verification"
        }
      ],
      "privacyPolicy": [
        {
          "policyURI": "https://example-bank.de/privacy/kyc",
          "type": "privacy_policy"
        }
      ],
      "credentials": [
        {
          "format": "dc+sd-jwt",
          "meta": "eu.europa.ec.eudi.pid.1",
          "claims": [
            {"path": ["credentialSubject", "family_name"]},
            {"path": ["credentialSubject", "given_name"]},
            {"path": ["credentialSubject", "birth_date"]}
          ]
        }
      ]
    }
  ]
}
```

</details>
<details>
<summary><strong>2. Registrar validates application</strong></summary>

The national Registrar systematically processes and validates the Relying Party's application against the policies mandated by eIDAS 2.0 (CIR 2025/848). This evaluation establishes the legal foundation for the Relying Party's operation within the EUDI ecosystem.

The validation routine executes four primary checks:

1. **Legal entity verification** — The Registrar cross-references the submitted `legalPerson`/`naturalPerson` identity data plus official `identifier` values against authoritative national sources (e.g., *Handelsregister* in Germany, *KVK* in the Netherlands) to ensure the Relying Party is a legally incorporated and active entity.
2. **Entitlement verification** — The Registrar confirms the Relying Party possesses the legal standing required for the registered `entitlements` and the declared requested-attestation scope in the `intendedUse` structures. For instance, a financial institution requesting PID data for onboarding under AML/KYC obligations must correspond to a valid banking or supervisory record in the relevant national authority dataset.
3. **Intended use assessment** — The Registrar enforces the GDPR's data minimisation principle (Art. 5(1)(c)) by strictly assessing whether each `credentials` / `claims` set is necessary and proportionate to the declared `purpose`.
4. **Intermediary status (if applicable)** — If the Relying Party designates itself as an intermediary (operating on behalf of other end-RPs), the Registrar verifies compliance with the specific no-storage and data-passthrough mandates articulated in eIDAS Art. 5b(10).

**Failure Path:** If the validation process detects fraud, mismatched credentials, or a severe policy violation, the Registrar rejects the application and terminates the process.

**Audit Telemetry:** The Registrar generates a `REG_APP_VALIDATION_FAILED` SIEM event upon detecting anomalous or non-compliant registration data.

```json
{
  "validation_status": "IN_PROGRESS",
  "checks": [
    {"type": "LEGAL_ENTITY", "status": "VERIFIED", "source": "National_Business_Registry"},
    {"type": "ENTITLEMENT", "status": "VERIFIED", "source": "Financial_Supervisory_Authority"},
    {"type": "DATA_MINIMISATION", "status": "PENDING_MANUAL_REVIEW"}
  ],
  "timestamp": "2026-06-15T09:30:00Z"
}
```

</details>
<details>
<summary><strong>3. Registrar confirms registration to Relying Party</strong></summary>

Upon successful validation, the Registrar commits the verified payload to the authoritative national register and exposes the resulting record through the signed query surface described in `§4.4`. In current TS5 terms, the durable cross-border anchor is the registered identifier set plus the `registryURI` of the published record, not a universally standardized article-specific `walletRelyingPartyId` field.

This activation marks the Relying Party as active (`status: ACTIVE`), and the Registrar exposes the verified data via the public Registrar REST API (`GET /wrp/{identifier}`). This endpoint acts as the authoritative source, enabling:

- **Wallet Units**: To dynamically query and render the Relying Party's verified identity and intended use cases on the user's consent screen during presentation flows.
- **Access CAs**: To verify the Relying Party's authorization status prior to minting any cryptographic WRPACs (Step 5).
- **Registration Cert Providers**: To retrieve the authoritative data required to issue offline WRPRCs (Step 11).
- **Supervisory Authorities**: To continuously monitor, audit, and regulate active Relying Parties.

**Failure Path:** If the Relying Party later violates ecosystem rules or GDPR mandates, the Registrar updates the authoritative register state and triggers the downstream suspension/cancellation and certificate-revocation cascade described in `§4.3.2`.

**Artifact Produced:** Authoritative register entry and `registryURI`.

**Audit Telemetry:** The Registrar logs `REGISTRATION_ACTIVATED` and propagates state changes to national notification services.

```http
HTTP/1.1 201 Created
Location: https://api.registrar.de/wrp/5299001GCLKH6FPVJW75

# Member State-specific acknowledgement body omitted intentionally:
# TS5 leaves POST response harmonisation for further study.
# The interoperable follow-up surface is the signed GET response on /wrp or /wrp/{identifier}.
```

> **Record Retention (Art. 10):** Registrars retain records of all Relying Party registration information—including all subsequent changes—for **10 years** (CIR 2025/848 Art. 10). This retention serves *ex post* monitoring, law enforcement investigations, and dispute handling. Relying Parties must ensure their entire registration history remains auditable.

</details>
<details>
<summary><strong>4. Relying Party requests WRPAC(s) from Access CA</strong></summary>

With a confirmed registration, the Relying Party must obtain its Wallet Relying Party Access Certificate (WRPAC). The Relying Party generates a secure Elliptic Curve key pair (typically P-256 or P-384) within its infrastructure (e.g., an HSM or secure enclave). 

The Relying Party constructs a standard PKCS#10 Certificate Signing Request (CSR). Crucially, this CSR binds the Relying Party's technical endpoint to its legal EUDI identity by carrying the registered subject identity that the Access CA will map into the WRPAC subject fields under ETSI TS 119 475 clause `5.1`, while also specifying the RP Instance's operational domain in the Subject Alternative Name (SAN).

**Failure Path:** If the CSR is malformed or uses an unsupported signature algorithm, the Access CA rejects the request and returns a `400 Bad Request`.

**Artifact Produced:** Cryptographic Key Pair (Private Key secured in HSM/KMS).

**Audit Telemetry:** The Relying Party logs a `WRPAC_CSR_GENERATED` SIEM event.

```text
Certificate Request:
    Data:
        Version: 1 (0x0)
        Subject: C=DE, O=Example Bank AG, serialNumber=5299001GCLKH6FPVJW75, CN=onboarding.example-bank.de
        Subject Public Key Info:
            Public Key Algorithm: id-ecPublicKey
                Public-Key: (256 bit)
                pub:
                    04:a1:b2:c3:d4... [EC Public Key]
        Attributes:
            Requested Extensions:
                X509v3 Subject Alternative Name: 
                    DNS:onboarding.example-bank.de
    Signature Algorithm: ecdsa-with-SHA256
         30:45:02:20:7c... [Signature]
```

> **One WRPAC per Relying Party Instance**: Each independent architectural component (e.g., the primary web backend, a distinct mobile API gateway, or distributed proximity terminals) requires its own WRPAC with isolated key material. While a single legal entity may hold dozens of WRPACs, the WRPAC's SAN domain must exactly match the `client_id` used in its OpenID4VP requests. This strict binding helps prevent domain spoofing and phishing attacks.

</details>
<details>
<summary><strong>5. Access CA queries Registrar to verify Relying Party registration</strong></summary>

The Access Certificate Authority (Access CA) operates under defined issuance policies and validates certificate requests using national registries. Upon receiving the CSR, the Access CA serves as a verification control point by querying the designated Registrar REST API (`GET /wrp/{identifier}`).

This verification step connects the PKI trust layer (X.509 cryptography) and the regulatory trust layer (national legal registration). The Access CA validates three mandatory conditions:
1. **Existence & Status**: The Relying Party must exist in the national registry and hold an `ACTIVE` status.
2. **Identifier Matching**: The registered RP identifier carried in the CSR subject data (for example an EUID or Member State-specific unique identifier) must match the identifier registered with the authority.
3. **Standing**: The Relying Party must not be flagged as suspended, revoked, or under investigation.

```http
GET /wrp/5299001GCLKH6FPVJW75 HTTP/1.1
Host: api.registrar.de
Authorization: Bearer [Access_CA_Privileged_Token]
Accept: application/jwt
```

**Failure Path:** If any condition fails, the Access CA immediately aborts the issuance process and logs the denied request. This safeguard helps ensure that unregistered, suspended, or unauthorized entities cannot obtain the cryptographic WRPACs necessary to interface with EUDI Wallet Units.

**Audit Telemetry:** The Access CA generates a `CA_REG_LOOKUP_DENIED` SIEM alert.

> **CA-Registrar binding**: This verification step is what connects the PKI trust layer (X.509 certificates) to the regulatory trust layer (national registration). A WRPAC without a backing Registrar entry is invalid by design.

</details>
<details>
<summary><strong>6. Registrar confirms registration to Access CA</strong></summary>

The Registrar responds to the same `/wrp/{identifier}` API call by returning the Relying Party's registered data points as a JWS-signed payload. In practice, the Access CA treats that signed register view as the authoritative confirmation that the RP is currently active and eligible for certificate issuance under the applicable national policy.

**Artifact Produced:** Registration Data Payload (JWS).

**Audit Telemetry:** The Access CA logs `REGISTRATION_CONFIRMED_BY_REGISTRAR`.

```http
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: application/jwt
x-jku-url: https://api.registrar.de/.well-known/jwks.json

eyJhbGciOiJFUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCIsImtpZCI6InJlZ2lzdHJhci0xIn0.
{
  "iss": "urn:eudi:registrar:de:bafin",
  "iat": 1718445123,
  "data": {
    "identifier": [
      {
        "type": "http://data.europa.eu/eudi/id/EUID",
        "identifier": "5299001GCLKH6FPVJW75"
      }
    ],
    "country": "DE",
    "legalPerson": {
      "legalName": ["Example Bank AG"]
    },
    "tradeName": "Example Bank",
    "registryURI": "https://api.registrar.de/wrp/5299001GCLKH6FPVJW75",
    "entitlements": [
      "https://uri.etsi.org/19475/Entitlement/Service_Provider"
    ]
  }
}.
[signature_bytes]
```

</details>
<details>
<summary><strong>7. Access CA issues X.509 Access Certificate</strong></summary>

The Access CA mints the X.509 Access Certificate. Crucially, the WRPAC binds the Relying Party's public key to its legal identity. It follows the ETSI TS 119 475 and ETSI TS 119 411-8 profile set, carrying the RP's registered subject identifier and country through the certificate subject / subject-alternative-name mapping rather than through an article-specific `walletRelyingPartyId` + `memberState` pair.

**Artifact Produced:** The X.509 Wallet Relying Party Access Certificate (WRPAC).

**Audit Telemetry:** The Access CA logs a `WRPAC_CERT_ISSUED` SIEM event.

```text
Certificate:
    Data:
        Version: 3 (0x2)
        Serial Number: 1234567890 (0x499602d2)
        Issuer: C=DE, O=Access CA GMBH, CN=EUDI WRPAC CA 1
        Subject: C=DE, O=Example Bank AG, serialNumber=5299001GCLKH6FPVJW75, CN=Example Bank EUDI Web Service
        X509v3 extensions:
            X509v3 Subject Alternative Name:
                DNS:onboarding.example-bank.de
```

</details>
<details>
<summary><strong>8. Access CA delivers WRPAC to Relying Party</strong></summary>

The Access CA cryptographically signs the X.509 Access Certificate and delivers the full certificate chain (Leaf → Intermediate CA → Root CA) back to the Relying Party. 

The Relying Party must immediately secure the corresponding private key within a resilient, hardware-backed key store (e.g., an HSM or managed Cloud KMS) to prevent key exfiltration. This private cryptographic material acts as the ultimate anchor for the Relying Party's authenticity, and is utilized across two primarily integration vectors:

- **Online Authentication**: To securely sign JSON-Secured Authorization Requests (JARs). The entire WRPAC chain is injected into the `x5c` header of the JWS payload, allowing remote Wallet Units to verify the signature.
- **Proximity Authentication**: To sign `readerAuth` structures during offline BLE/NFC mdoc presentations. The WRPAC is embedded directly in the COSE_Sign1 unprotected header.

```json
{
  "alg": "ES256",
  "typ": "oauth-authz-req+jwt",
  "x5c": [
    "MIIB... (WRPAC Leaf)",
    "MIIB... (Access CA Intermediate)",
    "MIIC... (Access CA Root)"
  ]
}
```

Because WRPACs carry high systemic risk if compromised, they possess a strictly limited validity period (typically 1–2 years). Relying Parties must engineer automated cryptographic rotation pipelines to renew WRPACs prior to expiration, as Wallet Units forcefully reject expired certificates at runtime.

</details>
<details>
<summary><strong>9. Access CA logs WRPAC to Certificate Transparency</strong></summary>

Where the applicable Access CA or ecosystem profile uses Certificate Transparency, the Access CA acts in the TSP-CA role and submits the newly minted WRPAC or its corresponding precertificate to one or more CT logs. The log returns a cryptographic Signed Certificate Timestamp (SCT), which is a commitment that the entry will be merged into the append-only log within the declared Maximum Merge Delay (MMD); inclusion and consistency proofs remain separate evidence surfaces.

This architectural requirement aligns the EUDI PKI with modern Web PKI standards and provides three critical security properties:

1. **Ecosystem-wide Auditability**: CT logs give monitors, auditors, Wallet operators, and RPs a shared visibility surface for detecting rogue, mis-issued, or compromised WRPACs.
2. **Additional Transparency**: Public logging creates an auditable issuance trail tying the Access CA, the RP identity, and the transparency evidence together, making unauthorized issuance easier to investigate.
3. **Policy Input, Not a Universal Wallet Rule**: Some ecosystem profiles may define SCT-count/diversity or later proof expectations, but ETSI lower-layer sources still do not establish a universal wallet hard-fail rule from SCT presence alone.

**Failure Path:** Whether CT logging failure blocks certificate delivery depends on the applicable Access CA policy, the lower-layer baseline, and the ecosystem rulebook rather than ETSI TS 119 411-8 alone. Temporary log outage, MMD delay, or delayed inclusion evidence is not automatically the same failure mode as rogue issuance.

**Artifact Produced:** Signed Certificate Timestamp (SCT).

**Audit Telemetry:** The Access CA logs a `WRPAC_CT_LOGGED` SIEM event.

```text
Certificate Extensions:
    CT Precertificate SCTs: 
        Signed Certificate Timestamp:
            Version   : v1 (0x0)
            Log ID    : 5c:a1:... (EUDI Public Log)
            Timestamp : Jun 15 14:26:00 2026 GMT
            Extensions: none
            Signature : ecdsa-with-SHA256
                        30:45:02:20:12...
```

> **Scope boundary**: Do not infer a universal wallet hard-fail rule from ETSI alone. Some ecosystems may require SCT presence or equivalent transparency evidence, while others may use CT primarily for monitoring and audit.

</details>
<details>
<summary><strong>10. Relying Party requests WRPRC from Registration Cert Provider</strong></summary>

While the WRPAC authenticates the presenter, it does not carry the full transparency and authorization surface that the wallet may need to evaluate. If the Member State supports WRPRCs, the relying party requests one from an authorized WRPRC provider so that its register-backed service descriptions, entitlements, credential-query scope, and purpose information can travel in a signed portable artifact.

The engineering value is not just "offline mode." A WRPRC lets the wallet inspect the relying party's declared scope using a signed token that is expected to be presented **together with** the WRPAC, instead of forcing a synchronous registrar lookup in every interaction.

**Artifact Produced:** WRPRC Data Request Payload.

**Audit Telemetry:** The Relying Party logs a `WRPRC_REQUEST_INITIATED` SIEM event.

```http
POST /wrprc/request HTTP/1.1
Host: certificates.bundesdruckerei.de
Content-Type: application/json
Authorization: Bearer [RP_Service_Token]

{
  "identifier": "5299001GCLKH6FPVJW75"
}
```

</details>
<details>
<summary><strong>11. Registration Cert Provider queries Registrar for Relying Party data</strong></summary>

To construct the WRPRC accurately, the Registration Cert Provider must source the current authoritative register data for the relying party. Under ETSI TS 119 475 clause `6.2.2.2`, the provider verifies that the relying party is listed in the national register, that the requested certificate contents match the register data, and that the relying party already holds at least one valid WRPAC.

The Provider therefore executes a direct query to the national Registrar API, targeting the relying party's unique identifier.

```http
GET /wrp/urn:eudi:wrp:de:bank-example:12345 HTTP/1.1
Host: api.registrar.de
Accept: application/jwt
Authorization: Bearer [RCP_Privileged_Token]
```

This strict backend-to-backend integration guarantees that the WRPRC is minted from current register state rather than static user-supplied inputs. If the national policy uses registrar-initiated issuance, the provider may also rely on registrar-side identity proofing instead of re-running a full identity-proofing cycle for every WRPRC issuance.

**Failure Path:** If the Relying Party's registration is suspended or not found, the Registrar returns a `404 Not Found` or `403 Forbidden`, and the Provider aborts issuance.

**Audit Telemetry:** The Provider generates a `RCP_REG_LOOKUP_DENIED` SIEM alert if the lookup is unsuccessful.

</details>
<details>
<summary><strong>12. Registrar returns registration data to Registration Cert Provider</strong></summary>

The Registrar returns a JWS-signed registration payload containing the relying party's current register data. The WRPRC provider then maps those register fields into the ETSI TS 119 475 WRPRC payload model: trade name and identifiers become `name` and `sub`, multilingual service descriptions become `srv_description`, entitlements become `entitlements`, and `intendedUse` structures become the service-provider `credentials`, `purpose`, and `intended_use_id` fields where relevant.

**Artifact Produced:** Registration Data Payload (JWS).

**Audit Telemetry:** The Registrar logs `REGISTRATION_DATA_SERVED_TO_RCP`.

```http
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: application/jwt
x-jku-url: https://api.registrar.de/.well-known/jwks.json

eyJhbGciOiJFUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCIsImtpZCI6InJlZ2lzdHJhci0xIn0.
{
  "iss": "urn:eudi:registrar:de:bafin",
  "iat": 1718446900,
  "data": {
    "identifier": [
      {
        "type": "http://data.europa.eu/eudi/id/EUID",
        "identifier": "529900T8BM49AURSDO55"
      }
    ],
    "country": "DE",
    "legalPerson": {
      "legalName": ["Example Bank AG"]
    },
    "tradeName": "Example Bank",
    "registryURI": "https://registrar.example-ms.de/wrp/529900T8BM49AURSDO55",
    "entitlements": [
      "https://uri.etsi.org/19475/Entitlement/Service_Provider",
      "https://uri.etsi.org/19475/SubEntitlement/psp/psp-as"
    ],
    "supportURI": [
      "https://support.example-bank.de/eudi-wallet"
    ],
    "srvDescription": [
      [
        {
          "lang": "en",
          "content": "Customer onboarding and retail banking"
        }
      ]
    ],
    "supervisoryAuthority": {
      "name": "Der Bundesbeauftragte fuer den Datenschutz und die Informationsfreiheit",
      "country": "DE",
      "email": [
        "poststelle@bfdi.bund.de"
      ],
      "formURI": [
        "https://www.bfdi.bund.de/DE/Service/Kontakt/kontakt_node.html"
      ]
    },
    "usesIntermediary": [],
    "isPSB": false,
    "isIntermediary": false,
    "policy": [
      {
        "policyURI": "https://example-bank.de/privacy/eudi-wallet",
        "type": "privacy_policy"
      }
    ],
    "intendedUse": [
      {
        "intendedUseIdentifier": "urn:eudi:wrp:de:example-bank:kyc",
        "purpose": [
          {
            "lang": "en",
            "content": "Customer onboarding and anti-money-laundering checks"
          }
        ],
        "privacyPolicy": [
          {
            "policyURI": "https://example-bank.de/privacy/eudi-wallet",
            "type": "http://data.europa.eu/eudi/policy/privacy-policy"
          }
        ],
        "credentials": [
          {
            "format": "dc+sd-jwt",
            "meta": "eu.europa.ec.eudi.pid.1",
            "claims": [
              {"path": ["credentialSubject", "family_name"]},
              {"path": ["credentialSubject", "given_name"]},
              {"path": ["credentialSubject", "birth_date"]}
            ]
          }
        ]
      }
    ]
  }
}.
[signature_bytes]
```

</details>
<details>
<summary><strong>13. Registration Cert Provider issues WRPRC</strong></summary>

The Registration Cert Provider now mints the WRPRC using the published ETSI TS 119 475 profile: JWT or CWT outer format, AdES B-B signature handling, and the payload structure defined in Tables `7–10` plus the lifecycle additions from clause `6.2.6.1`.

For a service-provider example, that means the certificate carries:

- Base transparency fields such as `name`, `sub`, `country`, `registry_uri`, `srv_description`, `entitlements`, `privacy_policy`, `info_uri`, `support_uri`, `supervisory_authority`, `policy_id`, `certificate_policy`, and `iat`
- Service-provider authorization fields such as `credentials`, `purpose`, and `intended_use_id`
- Lifecycle/status fields such as `exp`, `id`, and the status-list reference used for later validity checks

**Artifact Produced:** The Wallet Relying Party Registration Certificate (WRPRC JWT).

**Audit Telemetry:** The Provider logs `WRPRC_ISSUED`.

```json
{
  "alg": "ES256",
  "typ": "rc-wrp+jwt",
  "x5c": [
    "...provider leaf...",
    "...provider intermediate..."
  ]
}
.
{
  "name": "Example Bank",
  "sub_ln": "Example Bank AG",
  "sub": "LEIXG-529900T8BM49AURSDO55",
  "country": "DE",
  "registry_uri": "https://registrar.example-ms.de/wrp/529900T8BM49AURSDO55",
  "srv_description": [
    [
      {
        "lang": "en-US",
        "value": "Customer onboarding and retail banking"
      }
    ]
  ],
  "entitlements": [
    "https://uri.etsi.org/19475/Entitlement/Service_Provider",
    "https://uri.etsi.org/19475/SubEntitlement/psp/psp-as"
  ],
  "privacy_policy": "https://example-bank.de/privacy/eudi-wallet",
  "info_uri": "https://example-bank.de/eudi-wallet-info",
  "support_uri": "https://support.example-bank.de/eudi-wallet",
  "supervisory_authority": {
    "email": "poststelle@bfdi.bund.de",
    "phone": "+49-228-997799-0",
    "uri": "https://www.bfdi.bund.de/"
  },
  "policy_id": [
    "0.4.0.19475.3.1"
  ],
  "certificate_policy": "https://certificates.example-ms.de/wrprc-policy",
  "iat": 1718447800,
  "exp": 1726223800,
  "id": "urn:wrprc:example-ms:529900T8BM49AURSDO55:2026-06",
  "status": {
    "idx": "4812",
    "uri": "https://certificates.example-ms.de/status/wrprc/2026-06"
  },
  "credentials": [
    {
      "format": "dc+sd-jwt",
      "meta": {
        "vct_values": [
          "eu.europa.ec.eudi.pid.1"
        ]
      },
      "claim": [
        {
          "path": [
            "family_name"
          ]
        },
        {
          "path": [
            "given_name"
          ]
        },
        {
          "path": [
            "birth_date"
          ]
        }
      ]
    }
  ],
  "purpose": [
    {
      "lang": "en-US",
      "value": "Customer onboarding and anti-money-laundering checks"
    }
  ],
  "intended_use_id": "urn:eudi:wrp:de:example-bank:kyc"
}
.[signature]
```

The important lifecycle constraint is not an arbitrary short-term refresh heuristic. ETSI TS 119 475 instead says that `exp` must be no later than 12 months after `iat`, renewal re-checks register attributes, and the provider revokes or updates status when the national register changes in ways that make the WRPRC inaccurate.

</details>
<details>
<summary><strong>14. Registration Cert Provider delivers WRPRC to Relying Party</strong></summary>

The finalized WRPRC is securely delivered back to the relying party. The RP caches it alongside its WRPAC and includes it in presentation flows where the ecosystem profile expects the wallet to evaluate portable authorization data rather than perform a live registrar lookup.

Depending on the modality, the Relying Party transmits the WRPRC to Wallet Units via distinct architectural channels:
- **Online SD-JWT flows**: Embedded natively within the `client_metadata` payload inside the authorization JAR.
- **Proximity mdoc flows**: Encoded as a CBOR byte string and attached directly to the `readerAuth` COSE_Sign1 unprotected header during BLE/NFC engagement.

```json
{
  "client_id": "urn:eudi:wrp:de:bank-example:12345",
  "client_metadata": {
    "jwks": { "keys": [...] },
    "authorization_encrypted_response_alg": "ECDH-ES",
    "authorization_encrypted_response_enc": "A256GCM",
    "vp_formats": { "dc+sd-jwt": {}, "mso_mdoc": {} },
    "wallet_relying_party_registration_certificate": "eyJhbGciOiJFUzI1Ni...[WRPRC JWT]..."
  }
}
```

```http
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: application/jwt

eyJhbGciOiJFUzI1NiIsInR...[WRPRC JWT carrying entitlements, credentials, purpose, and status reference]
```

When the Wallet Unit processes the request, it validates the WRPRC provider's signature chain, reads the portable authorization fields (`entitlements`, `credentials`, `purpose`, `support_uri`, `srv_description`, and related transparency metadata), and checks the referenced status list. This lets the wallet evaluate registered scope without treating the WRPRC like an X.509 certificate with OCSP/CRL semantics.

> **WRPRC freshness model**: The right operational model is not "blind monthly refresh." The RP should renew before `exp`, but correctness is primarily enforced by register-backed re-issuance and by the provider's obligation to update the public status list without undue delay when the registration changes.

</details>

##### 4.3.2 Suspension and Cancellation of Registration (Art. 9)

CIR 2025/848 Art. 9 establishes three distinct pathways through which an RP's registration may be suspended or cancelled:

1. **Supervisory body request** (Art. 9(1)) — A supervisory body exercising its powers under Art. 46a(4)(f) of Regulation (EU) No 910/2014 may direct the Registrar to suspend or cancel the registration. This is a mandatory instruction — the Registrar **shall** comply.

2. **Registrar-initiated** (Art. 9(2)) — The Registrar **may** suspend or cancel a registration where it has reason to believe any of the following:
   - **(a)** The registration contains information that is inaccurate, out of date, or misleading
   - **(b)** The RP is not compliant with the registration policy
   - **(c)** The RP is requesting more attributes than it registered under Art. 5 and Art. 6 — **this is the data minimisation enforcement lever** (see [§9.1](#91-flow-description) for over-requesting risks)
   - **(d)** The RP is otherwise acting in breach of Union or national law in a manner related to its role as a wallet-relying party

3. **RP self-request** (Art. 9(3)) — The RP itself requests suspension or cancellation (e.g., when discontinuing a wallet-integrated service). The Registrar **shall** comply.

##### Proportionality Assessment

Before acting on registrar-initiated grounds (pathway 2), the Registrar must conduct a **proportionality assessment** (Art. 9(4)), weighing:

- Impact on users' fundamental rights, security, and confidentiality
- Severity of the disruption the suspension/cancellation would cause
- Associated costs for both the RP and its users

Based on this assessment, the Registrar may suspend or cancel with or without prior notice. This means an RP may find its registration suspended without warning if the breach is severe enough to justify immediate action.

##### 24-Hour Notification Cascade

When a registration is suspended or cancelled, Art. 9(5) mandates a strict notification timeline — the Registrar must inform all three parties **within 24 hours**:

1. **Provider of WRPAC** (Access CA) — to trigger certificate revocation
2. **Provider of WRPRC** (Registration Cert Provider) — to trigger certificate revocation
3. **The affected RP** — including the reasons and available means of redress/appeal

##### Certificate Revocation Cascade

Art. 9(6) mandates that upon suspension or cancellation, both the Access CA and Registration Cert Provider must **revoke without undue delay** all WRPACs and WRPRCs respectively belonging to the affected RP. This effectively paralyses the RP's ability to interact with any Wallet Unit — neither online (WRPAC) nor offline (WRPRC) verification will succeed.

> ⚠️ **RP Risk — Over-Requesting**: Art. 9(2)(c) explicitly makes requesting attributes beyond the registered scope a ground for suspension. This directly connects the data minimisation obligations in [§9.1](#91-flow-description) to a concrete enforcement consequence. RPs must ensure their OpenID4VP/DCQL queries never exceed their registered intended-use, credential, and claim scope, or they face suspension and loss of all wallet access.

#### 4.4 Registrar REST API

TS5 defines a public REST API (OpenAPI 3.1) enabling any party — including Wallet Units, RPs, and the general public — to query the national register of Wallet-Relying Parties. The API is critical for the RP integration flow because:

1. **Wallet Units query it** to verify RP registration and authoritative intended-use / credential scope when no WRPRC is available
2. **RPs query it** to verify their own registration status and that of intermediaries
3. **Supervisory bodies** use it for monitoring and enforcement

##### 4.4.1 API Endpoints

| Method | Path | Authentication | Purpose |
|:-------|:-----|:---------------|:--------|
| `GET` | `/wrp` | Public (open) | Query registered WRPs with optional filtering and pagination |
| `GET` | `/wrp/{identifier}` | Public (open) | Retrieve a single signed WRP record by identifier |
| `GET` | `/wrp/check-intended-use` | Public (open) | Optional dedicated boolean check for intended-use / credential / policy scope |
| `PUT` | `/wrp` | Authenticated (MS-specific) | Update an existing WRP registration |
| `DELETE` | `/wrp/{identifier}` | Authenticated (MS-specific) | Delete a WRP registration by identifier |
| `POST` | `/wrp` | Authenticated (MS-specific, not harmonized) | Intended create path, but still parked for further study in the current TS5 OpenAPI package |

> **Key design decision:** The stable interoperability surface today is the signed `GET` interface plus authenticated update/delete methods. TS5 still leaves `POST /wrp` harmonization for further study, so new-registration onboarding remains a Member State-specific process even when the published register data later converges on the shared TS2/TS5/TS6 model.

##### 4.4.2 Public Query Parameters

The current TS5 package splits its API detail between prose and the published OpenAPI 3.1 file. The parameter names below follow the current OpenAPI file, which is the safest concrete integration surface for query construction.

**`GET /wrp` parameters**

| Parameter | Type | Description |
|:----------|:-----|:------------|
| `identifier` | string | Official or business registration number |
| `legalname` | string | Official company name |
| `tradename` | string | Trade name |
| `policy` | string | URL of the WRP's privacy policy |
| `entitlement` | string | Type of entitlement (e.g., PuB-EAA Provider) |
| `credentialmeta` | string | Filter by the metadata value of a requested credential |
| `credentialformat` | string | Narrow `credentialmeta` matches to a specific credential format |
| `usesintermediary` | string | Filter by intermediary identifier |
| `isintermediary` | boolean | Whether WRP acts as an intermediary |
| `intendeduseidentifier` | string | Identifier for a specific intended use |
| `limit` | integer | Page size for cursor-based pagination |
| `cursor` | string | Cursor for the next page of results |

**`GET /wrp/check-intended-use` parameters**

| Parameter | Type | Required | Description |
|:----------|:-----|:--------:|:------------|
| `rpidentifier` | string | ✅ | Unique identifier of the WRP being checked |
| `intendeduseidentifier` | string | | Registrar-level identifier of the intended use |
| `credentialformat` | string | | Credential format to check (e.g., `dc+sd-jwt`) |
| `claimpath` | string | | Claim path to verify within the intended use |
| `credentialmeta` | string | | Optional credential metadata value for a narrower check |
| `policyurl` | string | | Privacy-policy URL check against the registered intended use |

Parameters can be combined:

```http
GET /wrp?identifier=5299001GCLKH6FPVJW75&credentialformat=dc+sd-jwt&credentialmeta=eu.europa.ec.eudi.pid.1
GET /wrp?legalname=Another%20Org&isintermediary=false
GET /wrp/check-intended-use?rpidentifier=5299001GCLKH6FPVJW75&credentialformat=dc%2Bsd-jwt&claimpath=family_name
```

##### 4.4.3 Response Format

All `GET` responses are **JWS-signed** by the Registrar and returned as `application/jwt`.

- `GET /wrp` returns a `SignedWRPArray` payload.
- `GET /wrp/{identifier}` returns a `SignedWRP` payload.
- `GET /wrp/check-intended-use` returns a `SignedIntendedUseCheckResult` payload.

TS5 prose also expects the register query surface to provide WRPAC Certificate Transparency-related information for matching records where applicable. The published OpenAPI file does not model a standardized CT-history container explicitly, so implementations may expose any such data as an extension around the core signed WRP data rather than as a guaranteed interoperable baseline object.

Where a Member State exposes the TS5-style `GET /wrp/check-intended-use` endpoint, it returns a JWS-signed boolean `TRUE` or `FALSE`. This is a lightweight optimization, not the only model a wallet can use during presentation; wallets can also retrieve the broader register record and evaluate scope locally.

##### 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

When a Wallet Unit receives a presentation request from an RP Instance that does not include a WRPRC, the Wallet Unit uses the Registrar API to verify the RP's authoritative registration and request scope:

```mermaid
---
config:
  themeVariables:
    noteBkgColor: "transparent"
    noteBorderColor: "transparent"
  sequence:
    messageAlign: left
    noteAlign: left
    actorMargin: 250
---
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant WU as 📱 Wallet Unit
    participant RPI as 🏦 RP Instance
    participant REG as 🏛️ Registrar API

    RPI->>WU: Presentation request (WRPAC, no WRPRC)
    WU->>WU: Authenticate RP Instance via WRPAC
    Note right of WU: User enables "verify<br/>registration" check

    WU->>REG: GET /wrp/{identifier}<br/>or equivalent scope query
    REG-->>WU: JWS-signed registration<br/>or scope response
    
    alt Registered scope verified
        WU->>WU: Show User: "RP registered for<br/>these attributes ✅"
    else Scope mismatch or registration unverifiable
        WU->>WU: Show User: "⚠️ RP requested attributes<br/>not matching registration"
    Note right of REG: ⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀
    end
    
    WU->>WU: User approves/denies

    Note right of REG: ⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀
```

<details>
<summary><strong>1. Relying Party sends presentation request to Wallet Unit</strong></summary>

The Relying Party Instance initiates the presentation flow by transmitting a request to the user's Wallet Unit. Depending on the architectural modality, this is either an OpenID4VP JSON-secured Authorization Request (JAR) traversing the internet, or an ISO/IEC 18013-5 `DeviceRequest` transmitted over BLE/NFC. 

To cryptographically assert its identity, the Relying Party embeds its Wallet Relying Party Access Certificate (WRPAC). 

```json
{
  "client_id": "urn:eudi:wrp:de:bank-example:12345",
  "client_metadata": {
    "jwks": {
      "keys": [{
        "kty": "EC",
        "crv": "P-256",
        "x": "...",
        "y": "...",
        "x5c": ["MIIC...WRPAC...", "MIIC...Intermediate..."]
      }]
    }
  }
}
```

In this specific scenario, the Relying Party **omits** the Wallet Relying Party Registration Certificate (WRPRC). This omission might occur because the Relying Party's Member State has not deployed a Registration Cert Provider infrastructure, or because the relying party prefers to let the wallet fetch current register data online. Because the WRPRC is missing, the Wallet Unit lacks the portable authorization and transparency payload (`entitlements`, `credentials`, `purpose`, `intended_use_id`, support metadata) and is forced into an online execution path (Steps 3–4) to query the national Registrar.

**Audit Telemetry:** The Wallet Unit logs an `INBOUND_PRESENTATION_REQUEST_RECEIVED` event.

> **Offline Bypass**: If the Relying Party *had* embedded a WRPRC, the Wallet Unit could parse the portable `credentials`, `purpose`, `entitlements`, and related transparency metadata directly from the certificate payload, entirely bypassing the synchronous REST API network hops to the Registrar and skipping straight to user consent.

</details>
<details>
<summary><strong>2. Wallet Unit authenticates Relying Party Instance via WRPAC</strong></summary>

Upon receiving the presentation request, the Wallet Unit enters a cryptographic verification phase. It must verify that the Relying Party operating the endpoint (`client_id`) legally owns the private key that signed the request.

The Wallet Unit extracts the WRPAC leaf certificate from the request (e.g., from the JAR's `x5c` header or the mdoc `readerAuth` structure) and executes the following path validation:

1. **Chain Building**: The Wallet Unit constructs the certification path from the WRPAC leaf, through the Access CA intermediate, terminating at a trusted Root CA listed in the national List of Trusted Entities (LoTE).
2. **Signature Verification**: The Wallet Unit verifies the ECDSA signatures across the entire chain.
3. **Status Check**: The Wallet Unit verifies the certificate's validity period and queries its revocation status (via OCSP stapling, CRLs, or cached offline lists).
4. **Identity Binding**: The Wallet Unit extracts the RP identifier from the WRPAC's subject / SAN mapping and correlates it with the presentation `client_id`.

```text
Subject:
    serialNumber=5299001GCLKH6FPVJW75
X509v3 Subject Alternative Name:
    DNS:onboarding.example-bank.de
```

This procedure authenticates **who** the Relying Party is, but crucially, it does not validate **what they are legally permitted to request**. The WRPAC proves the Relying Party is a registered entity capable of holding keys, but determining which credentials, claims, and declared purposes are in scope requires WRPRC or register evaluation.

**Failure Path:** If any dimension of the WRPAC validation fails (chain broken, expired, revoked, or untrusted Root), the Wallet MUST reject the presentation request. 

**Audit Telemetry:** The Wallet Unit triggers a `WRPAC_VALIDATION_FAILED` security event upon rejection.

> **Hard Protocol Termination**: An unauthenticated Relying Party represents a security risk, irrespective of the attributes it is attempting to access.

</details>
<details>
<summary><strong>3. Wallet Unit queries Registrar API for authoritative scope data</strong></summary>

The Wallet Unit constructs an HTTP GET request to the Registrar API to retrieve authoritative scope information for the Relying Party. ETSI TS 119 475 does not require a specific wallet-to-register API shape here. In current TS5-style models, some Member States may expose a lightweight `check-intended-use` endpoint, but the more general and future-proof model is to retrieve the relevant register object and evaluate the credential/claim scope from that signed response.

```http
GET /wrp/5299001GCLKH6FPVJW75 HTTP/1.1
Host: registrar.example-ms.de
Accept: application/jwt
```

The Wallet correlates the returned register object with the presentation request's credential format, credential metadata, requested claim paths, and any intended-use identifier carried in the request. A Member State may choose to offer a lighter boolean endpoint as an optimization, but the authoritative model remains the register record plus wallet-side scope evaluation. The Registrar endpoint must be reachable over TLS 1.3, and the Wallet Unit should verify the Registrar's TLS certificate chain. See [§4.4.5](#445-api-payload-walkthrough-ts5-openapi-31) for the current TS5-style endpoint examples.

**Audit Telemetry:** The Wallet Unit logs a `REGISTRAR_INTENDED_USE_QUERY_INITIATED` event.

> **Offline fallback**: If the Registrar is unreachable (no internet, DNS failure, timeout), the Wallet Unit should inform the User: *"Registration check unavailable — proceed at your own risk"*, and allow the User to decide. The Wallet Unit should NOT silently proceed without the check.

</details>
<details>
<summary><strong>4. Registrar API returns signed registration scope data</strong></summary>

The Registrar API returns a `200 OK` response with `Content-Type: application/jwt` — a JWS compact serialisation signed with the Registrar's private key. The `x-jku-url` response header points to the Registrar's JWKS for signature verification. The JWS payload can carry the registered WRP data needed for the wallet to determine whether the request is in scope:

```json
{
  "iss": "urn:eudi:registrar:de:bafin",
  "iat": 1750003200,
  "data": {
    "identifier": [
      {
        "type": "http://data.europa.eu/eudi/id/EUID",
        "identifier": "5299001GCLKH6FPVJW75"
      }
    ],
    "country": "DE",
    "legalPerson": {
      "legalName": ["Example Bank AG"]
    },
    "tradeName": "Example Bank",
    "entitlements": [
      "https://uri.etsi.org/19475/Entitlement/Service_Provider"
    ],
    "srvDescription": [
      [
        {"lang": "en", "content": "Online banking and account opening"}
      ]
    ],
    "supportURI": ["https://support.example-bank.de/eudi-wallet"],
    "supervisoryAuthority": {
      "name": "Der Bundesbeauftragte fuer den Datenschutz und die Informationsfreiheit",
      "country": "DE",
      "email": ["poststelle@bfdi.bund.de"]
    },
    "intendedUse": [
      {
        "intendedUseIdentifier": "urn:eudi:wrp:de:example-bank:kyc",
        "purpose": [
          {"lang": "en", "content": "Customer identification and KYC verification"}
        ],
        "credentials": [
          {
            "format": "dc+sd-jwt",
            "meta": "eu.europa.ec.eudi.pid.1",
            "claims": [
              {"path": ["credentialSubject", "family_name"]},
              {"path": ["credentialSubject", "given_name"]},
              {"path": ["credentialSubject", "birth_date"]}
            ]
          }
        ]
      }
    ]
  }
}
```

The Wallet Unit MUST verify the JWS signature before trusting the response — an unsigned or tampered response could lead to the Wallet Unit incorrectly approving an out-of-scope request. The Wallet Unit fetches the Registrar's JWKS from the `x-jku-url` header (or from a cached copy) and verifies the signature using the matching `kid`. It then compares the requested credential formats, claim paths, and intended-use identifier against the returned `intendedUse` structures. A Member State that exposes a TS5-style boolean endpoint may collapse this evaluation into a direct `true` / `false` response, but that is an optimization rather than the only valid model.

**Artifact Produced:** Registrar Verification Payload (JWS).

**Failure Path:** If the JWS signature verification fails, the Wallet Unit rejects the payload as tampered and halts the presentation flow.

**Audit Telemetry:** The Wallet Unit logs a `REGISTRAR_RESPONSE_VERIFIED_SUCCESS` or `REGISTRAR_RESPONSE_VERIFICATION_FAILED` event.

> **JWS signing rationale**: The Registrar signs every response to prevent man-in-the-middle attacks where a network adversary could replace a `false` response with `true`, tricking the User into sharing data with an unregistered Relying Party.

</details>
<details>
<summary><strong>5. Wallet Unit displays verified Relying Party attributes to User</strong></summary>

If the Registrar API confirms the requested scope — either via a positive `isRegistered: true` result from `GET /wrp/check-intended-use` or via a signed register record whose `intendedUse` data matches the request — the Wallet Unit's UI transitions to the presentation consent screen, displaying positive trust indicators. This UI signifies that the Relying Party is registered and authorized to access the requested attributes.

The Wallet UI dynamically maps the validated data to the screen:
- **Verified Identity**: The Relying Party's `tradeName` (e.g., *"Example Bank AG"*) is rendered prominently, pulled from the WRPAC SAN or the cached Registrar profile.
- **Trust Seal**: A highly visible, standardized trust mark (e.g., ✅) accompanied by ecosystem-approved nomenclature like *"This service is officially registered and monitored by your national authority."*
- **Granular Transparency**: Each requested attribute (e.g., `family_name`, `birth_date`) is listed alongside the Relying Party's registered `purpose` (e.g., *"For anti-money laundering compliance (KYC)"*).

```json
{
  "ui_state": "TRUSTED",
  "rendered_elements": [
    {"type": "TRUST_BADGE", "status": "VERIFIED"},
    {"type": "ATTRIBUTE_LIST", "items": ["family_name", "birth_date"]},
    {"type": "PURPOSE_TEXT", "value": "Customer identification and KYC verification"}
  ]
}
```

This affirmative signal helps the User understand that the interaction is covered by eIDAS 2.0 regulatory oversight and that the request is within the registered scope.

</details>
<details>
<summary><strong>6. Wallet Unit displays severity warning for unmatched attributes to User</strong></summary>

If the Registrar API indicates a mismatch — either via an explicit `isRegistered: false` result or because the signed register record does not contain a matching intended-use / credential / claim combination — the Wallet Unit MUST interrupt the standard UX flow and render a warning.

This control is the fundamental defense mechanism against Relying Parties attempting to over-request data outside their legal purview (e.g., a bank requesting health indicators). 

The Wallet UI executes an explicit warning intervention:
- **Visual Alert**: The interface shifts to an amber or red warning state (⚠️).
- **Explicit Callout**: "This service is **NOT** authorized to request the highlighted data (e.g., `health_status`)."
- **Regulatory Context**: A "Learn More" hyperlink driving the User directly to the national Registrar's public portal, allowing the User to inspect the Relying Party's actual, limited registration record.

```json
{
  "ui_state": "WARNING_ELEVATED",
  "mismatched_claims": ["health_status", "location"],
  "warning_text": "The service may not have regulatory approval for this data — sharing is at your own risk."
}
```

Crucially, under the sovereignty principles of eIDAS 2.0 (Art. 5a(4)), this warning is strictly **informational, not blocking**. The Wallet Unit cannot unilaterally forbid the User from transmitting their own data. The User retains the final decision on whether to override the warning and force the presentation, but the UI ensures they do so aware of the Relying Party's non-compliance.

**Audit Telemetry:** The Wallet Unit logs an `UNMATCHED_ATTRIBUTE_WARNING_DISPLAYED` event.

</details>
<details>
<summary><strong>7. User authorizes or aborts credential presentation</strong></summary>

At this stage, the User reviews the UI trust indicators (✅ or ⚠️) and decides whether to authorize or abort the transaction. The European digital identity paradigm dictates that cryptographic transmission cannot occur without this explicit, granular consent step.

The Wallet Unit translates the User's choice into protocol-level actions:

- **If Approved (Full or Partial)**: The Wallet Unit synthesizes the finalized payload. For an OpenID4VP flow, it constructs the SD-JWT VP Token, signs the Key Binding JWT (KB-JWT) over the Relying Party's `nonce`, and POSTs the payload to the Relying Party's `response_uri`. If the User exercised selective disclosure (denying specific attributes like `age` while approving `name`), the Wallet Unit strips the corresponding disclosure strings from the presentation.
- **If Denied**: The Wallet Unit halts cryptographic generation. In online domains, it explicitly signals the rejection by dispatching an OAuth 2.0 `access_denied` error code to the Relying Party's backend, allowing the Relying Party to update its web session. In proximity environments, the Wallet Unit silently drops the BLE/NFC connection.

**Artifact Produced:** Verifiable Presentation Payload (e.g., SD-JWT VP Token or mdoc readerAuth).

**Audit Telemetry:** The Wallet Unit logs a `USER_CONSENT_DECISION_RECORDED` event.

```http
POST /api/eudi-response HTTP/1.1
Host: onboarding.example-bank.de
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

response=eyJhbGciOiJFUzI1NiIs...[VP Token Payload]...&state=af0ifjsldkj
```

> **Note — Partial Consent Is a Normal Outcome, Not an Error**
>
> Wallet Units increasingly support granular, per-attribute toggles. Relying Parties must engineer their backend verifier logic to handle presentations that arrive with missing, un-consented attributes. If a Relying Party rigidly terminates the session when a non-critical attribute is missing, it creates a poor user experience that undermines the ecosystem's data-minimization goals.

</details>

##### 4.4.5 API Payload Walkthrough (TS5 OpenAPI 3.1)

The query strings below follow the current TS5 OpenAPI 3.1 file (`ts5-openapi31-registrar-api.yml`), while the decoded `WalletRelyingParty` payloads follow the current TS5 JSON data model and prose-level class naming. All `GET` responses are returned as `application/jwt` (JWS compact serialisation) with an `x-jku-url` header pointing to the Registrar's JWKS.

<details>
<summary><strong>GET /wrp?identifier={id}</strong> — Query registered WRPs by identifier</summary>

```http
GET /wrp?identifier=5299001GCLKH6FPVJW75 HTTP/1.1
Host: registrar.example-ms.de
Accept: application/jwt
```

Response (`200 OK`, `Content-Type: application/jwt`, `x-jku-url: https://registrar.example-ms.de/.well-known/jwks.json`):

The JWS payload, once decoded and signature-verified against the Registrar's JWKS, conforms to the `SignedWRPArray` schema:

```json
{
  "iss": "urn:eudi:registrar:de:bafin",
  "iat": 1750003200,
  "data": [
    {
      "country": "DE",
      "identifier": [
        {
          "type": "http://data.europa.eu/eudi/id/EUID",
          "identifier": "5299001GCLKH6FPVJW75"
        }
      ],
      "legalPerson": {
        "legalName": ["Example Bank AG"]
      },
      "policy": [
        {
          "policyURI": "https://example-bank.de/privacy",
          "type": "privacy_policy"
        }
      ],
      "entitlements": [
        "https://uri.etsi.org/19475/Entitlement/Service_Provider",
        "https://uri.etsi.org/19475/SubEntitlement/psp/psp-as"
      ],
      "isPSB": false,
      "isIntermediary": false,
      "tradeName": "Example Bank",
      "registryURI": "https://registrar.example-ms.de/wrp/5299001GCLKH6FPVJW75",
      "supportURI": [
        "https://support.example-bank.de/eudi-wallet",
        "mailto:eudi-support@example-bank.de"
      ],
      "supervisoryAuthority": {
        "name": "Der Bundesbeauftragte fuer den Datenschutz und die Informationsfreiheit",
        "country": "DE",
        "email": ["poststelle@bfdi.bund.de"],
        "formURI": ["https://www.bfdi.bund.de/DE/Service/Kontakt/kontakt_node.html"]
      },
      "srvDescription": [
        [
          {"lang": "de", "content": "Online-Banking und Kontoeroeffnung"},
          {"lang": "en", "content": "Online banking and account opening"}
        ]
      ],
      "intendedUse": [
        {
          "intendedUseIdentifier": "urn:eudi:wrp:de:example-bank:kyc",
          "createdAt": "2026-06-15T10:30:00Z",
          "purpose": [
            {"lang": "de", "content": "Kundenidentifizierung und KYC-Prüfung"},
            {"lang": "en", "content": "Customer identification and KYC verification"}
          ],
          "privacyPolicy": [
            {
              "policyURI": "https://example-bank.de/privacy/kyc",
              "type": "privacy_policy"
            }
          ],
          "credentials": [
            {
              "format": "dc+sd-jwt",
              "meta": "eu.europa.ec.eudi.pid.1",
              "claims": [
                {"path": ["credentialSubject", "family_name"]},
                {"path": ["credentialSubject", "given_name"]},
                {"path": ["credentialSubject", "birth_date"]},
                {"path": ["credentialSubject", "age_over_18"]},
                {"path": ["credentialSubject", "nationality"]},
                {"path": ["credentialSubject", "resident_address"]}
              ]
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "intendedUseIdentifier": "urn:eudi:wrp:de:example-bank:sca",
          "createdAt": "2026-06-15T10:30:00Z",
          "purpose": [
            {"lang": "en", "content": "Strong Customer Authentication for payments"}
          ],
          "privacyPolicy": [
            {
              "policyURI": "https://example-bank.de/privacy/sca",
              "type": "privacy_policy"
            }
          ],
          "credentials": [
            {
              "format": "dc+sd-jwt",
              "meta": "eu.europa.ec.eudi.sca.payment.1",
              "claims": [
                {"path": ["credentialSubject", "pan_last_four"]},
                {"path": ["credentialSubject", "scheme"]}
              ]
            }
          ]
        }
      ],
      "usesIntermediary": []
    }
  ],
  "pagination": {
    "next_cursor": null,
    "has_next_page": false
  }
}
```


</details>
<details>
<summary><strong>GET /wrp/{identifier}</strong> — Retrieve a single WRP by identifier</summary>

```http
GET /wrp/5299001GCLKH6FPVJW75 HTTP/1.1
Host: registrar.example-ms.de
Accept: application/jwt
```

Response (`200 OK`): Same as the `GET /wrp` response, but the JWS payload conforms to `SignedWRP` (single object, not array):

```json
{
  "iss": "urn:eudi:registrar:de:bafin",
  "iat": 1750003200,
  "data": {
    "country": "DE",
    "identifier": [
      {
        "type": "http://data.europa.eu/eudi/id/EUID",
        "identifier": "5299001GCLKH6FPVJW75"
      }
    ],
    "legalPerson": {
      "legalName": ["Example Bank AG"]
    },
    "entitlements": [
      "https://uri.etsi.org/19475/Entitlement/Service_Provider"
    ],
    "isPSB": false,
    "isIntermediary": false,
    "tradeName": "Example Bank",
    "registryURI": "https://registrar.example-ms.de/wrp/5299001GCLKH6FPVJW75",
    "supportURI": ["https://support.example-bank.de/eudi-wallet"],
    "intendedUse": [ "..." ]
  }
}
```

`404 Not Found` if the identifier does not match any registered WRP.


</details>
<details>
<summary><strong>GET /wrp/check-intended-use</strong> — Lightweight boolean check (used by Wallet Units)</summary>

This is the lightweight boolean optimization endpoint in the current TS5-style model for real-time RP verification during presentation. Wallets may use it where available, but they can also retrieve the broader register object and evaluate scope locally.

```http
GET /wrp/check-intended-use?rpidentifier=5299001GCLKH6FPVJW75
    &credentialformat=dc%2Bsd-jwt
    &claimpath=family_name
    &credentialmeta=eu.europa.ec.eudi.pid.1
Host: registrar.example-ms.de
Accept: application/jwt
```

Response (`200 OK`): JWS payload conforming to `SignedIntendedUseCheckResult`:

```json
{
  "iss": "urn:eudi:registrar:de:bafin",
  "iat": 1750003200,
  "data": {
    "isRegistered": true,
    "details": "Intended use 'urn:eudi:wrp:de:example-bank:kyc' includes claim 'family_name' in format 'dc+sd-jwt' with meta 'eu.europa.ec.eudi.pid.1'"
  }
}
```

When the RP requests an attribute it is **not** registered for:

```json
{
  "iss": "urn:eudi:registrar:de:bafin",
  "iat": 1750003200,
  "data": {
    "isRegistered": false,
    "details": "No matching intended use found for claim 'IBAN' in format 'dc+sd-jwt'"
  }
}
```


</details>
<details>
<summary><strong>PUT /wrp</strong> — Update existing WRP registration (authenticated)</summary>

```http
PUT /wrp HTTP/1.1
Host: registrar.example-ms.de
Content-Type: application/json
Authorization: Bearer &lt;MS-specific auth token>

{
  "country": "DE",
  "identifier": [
    {
      "type": "http://data.europa.eu/eudi/id/EUID",
      "identifier": "5299001GCLKH6FPVJW75"
    }
  ],
  "legalPerson": {
    "legalName": ["Example Bank AG"]
  },
  "entitlements": [
    "https://uri.etsi.org/19475/Entitlement/Service_Provider"
  ],
  "isPSB": false,
  "isIntermediary": false,
  "tradeName": "Example Bank",
  "supportURI": [
    "https://support.example-bank.de/eudi-wallet",
    "mailto:eudi-support@example-bank.de",
    "tel:+49-800-123-4567"
  ],
  "supervisoryAuthority": {
    "name": "Der Bundesbeauftragte fuer den Datenschutz und die Informationsfreiheit",
    "country": "DE",
    "email": ["poststelle@bfdi.bund.de"]
  },
  "srvDescription": [
    [
      {"lang": "en", "content": "Online banking and account opening"}
    ]
  ],
  "intendedUse": [
    {
      "intendedUseIdentifier": "urn:eudi:wrp:de:example-bank:kyc",
      "purpose": [
        {"lang": "en", "content": "Customer identification and KYC verification"}
      ],
      "privacyPolicy": [
        {"policyURI": "https://example-bank.de/privacy/kyc", "type": "privacy_policy"}
      ],
      "credentials": [
        {
          "format": "dc+sd-jwt",
          "meta": "eu.europa.ec.eudi.pid.1",
          "claims": [
            {"path": ["credentialSubject", "family_name"]},
            {"path": ["credentialSubject", "given_name"]},
            {"path": ["credentialSubject", "birth_date"]},
            {"path": ["credentialSubject", "resident_address"]},
            {"path": ["credentialSubject", "resident_country"]}
          ]
        }
      ]
    }
  ]
}
```

Response (`200 OK`): The updated WRP as a JWS-signed `SignedWRP` object. `400` if the payload is invalid, `404` if the WRP does not exist.

> **Note**: The `POST` method for creating new registrations has been **parked for further study** by request of DE. Each Member State may implement a different registration application process.


</details>
<details>
<summary><strong>DELETE /wrp/{identifier}</strong> — Delete a WRP registration (authenticated)</summary>

```http
DELETE /wrp/5299001GCLKH6FPVJW75 HTTP/1.1
Host: registrar.example-ms.de
Authorization: Bearer &lt;MS-specific auth token>
```

Response: `204 No Content` on success, `404 Not Found` if the identifier does not match.


</details>

##### 4.4.6 Security Considerations

TS5 frames the following as implementation guidance and national-documentation expectations for the Registrar API:

- **DDoS protection**: Cloud-based DDoS protection, WAF, rate limiting
- **Rate limiting**: Per-IP rate limiting, with the concrete thresholds documented by the Member State implementation
- **Query complexity limits**: Maximum number of query parameters per call
- **Caching**: Aggressive caching for `GET` responses (registration data changes infrequently)
- **Network segmentation**: API servers in private subnet behind WAF, isolated from the Registrar database
- **Monitoring**: Request rates, latency, error rates, source IP alerting

---

### 5. Trust Infrastructure: Certificates, Attestations, and Trusted Lists

> **Important — RP trust evaluation is a distributed systems problem as much as a cryptography problem**
>
> This chapter is easy to read as “validate a few certificates,” but the operational reality is broader: Access CAs, Registrars, Trusted Lists, revocation endpoints, Certificate Transparency, issuer key rotation, and optional federation trust chains can each fail independently. Production RP designs need to treat trust verification as a multi-component runtime dependency with caching, fallback, monitoring, and explicit failure handling.

#### 5.1 Certificate Hierarchy and Trust Chains

The EUDI Wallet trust infrastructure is built on a hierarchical certificate model that enables Wallet Units to verify the identity and authorisation of every entity they interact with. For the RP, this hierarchy determines how the RP authenticates itself to Wallet Units and how the RP verifies the credentials it receives.

##### 5.1.1 End-to-End Trust Architecture

```mermaid
---
config:
  flowchart:
    subGraphTitleMargin:
      bottom: 25
    nodeSpacing: 50
    rankSpacing: 50
---
flowchart TD
    subgraph Commission["`**European Commission**`"]
        CI["`**Common Trust Infrastructure**
        Publishes URLs of all
        Trusted Lists and LoTEs`"]
    end

    subgraph MemberState["`**Member State**`"]
        TLP["`**Trusted List / LoTE Provider**
        Signs and publishes LoTEs
        containing trust anchors`"]
        REG["`**Registrar**
        Registers RPs, publishes
        registration data online`"]
        ACA["`**Access Certificate Authority**
        Issues WRPACs to RP Instances`"]
        RCP["`**Registration Cert Provider**
        Issues WRPRCs (optional)`"]
    end

    subgraph RPSystem["`**Relying Party**`"]
        RP["`**RP Legal Entity**
        Registered with Registrar`"]
        RPI1["`**RP Instance 1**
        Web service (remote)`"]
        RPI2["`**RP Instance 2**
        Physical terminal (proximity)`"]
    end

    subgraph WalletSide["`**Wallet Ecosystem**`"]
        WU["`**Wallet Unit**
        Verifies RP certificates`"]
        WP["`**Wallet Provider**
        Issues WUA/WIA`"]
    end

    CI -->|"Publishes LoTE URLs"| TLP
    TLP -->|"Trust anchors"| ACA
    TLP -->|"Trust anchors"| WU
    REG -->|"Registration confirmed"| ACA
    REG -->|"Registration data"| RCP
    ACA -->|"WRPAC"| RPI1
    ACA -->|"WRPAC"| RPI2
    RCP -->|"WRPRC"| RP
    RPI1 -->|"`WRPAC + presentation
    request`"| WU
    RPI2 -->|"`WRPAC + presentation
    request`"| WU
    WU -->|"`Validate WRPAC against
    trust anchor from LoTE`"| WU
    WP -->|"`WUA (to PID/Attestation
    Providers only)`"| WU
    REG -->|"Public API"| WU

    style CI text-align:left
    style TLP text-align:left
    style REG text-align:left
    style ACA text-align:left
    style RCP text-align:left
    style RP text-align:left
    style RPI1 text-align:left
    style RPI2 text-align:left
    style WU text-align:left
    style WP text-align:left
```

##### 5.1.2 Certificate Chain for RP Authentication

When a Wallet Unit processes a presentation request from an RP Instance, it performs the following certificate chain validation:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TA["`**Trust Anchor**
    In LoTE, signed by
    Trusted&nbsp;List&nbsp;Provider`"]
    INT["`**Intermediate CA**
    Certificate(s)
    (optional)`"]
    WRPAC["`**WRPAC**
    Wallet-Relying Party
    Access&nbsp;Certificate`"]
    SIG["`**Signature**
    Over the
    presentation&nbsp;request`"]
    TA --> INT --> WRPAC --> SIG
    style TA text-align:left
    style INT text-align:left
    style WRPAC text-align:left
    style SIG text-align:left
```

The Wallet Unit:

1. Extracts the WRPAC and any intermediate certificates from the presentation request
2. Verifies the signature over the request using the public key in the WRPAC
3. Validates the certificate chain up to the trust anchor obtained from the LoTE
4. Checks revocation status for every certificate in the chain (including the trust anchor if applicable)
5. If all checks pass, the RP Instance is authenticated

##### 5.1.3 Trust Verification from RP Side

When the RP receives a presentation response, it performs its own trust chain validation:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph PID["PID/QEAA Trust Path"]
        TA1["`**PID/QEAA Trust Anchor**
        In LoTE/Trusted List, via
        Commission infrastructure`"]
        
        INT1["`**Intermediate signing certificate(s)**
        (optional)`"]
        
        ISS1["`**PID/QEAA Issuer signing key**`"]
        
        SIG1["`**Signature** over the PID/attestation`"]
        
        TA1 --> INT1 --> ISS1 --> SIG1
    end

    subgraph PUB["PuB-EAA Trust Path"]
        TA2["`**PuB-EAA Trust Anchor**
        QTSP Trusted List`"]
        
        QT["`**QTSP signing certificate**`"]
        
        PPC["`**PuB-EAA Provider certificate**`"]
        
        SIG2["`**Signature** over the PuB-EAA`"]
        
        TA2 --> QT --> PPC --> SIG2
    end

    PID ~~~ PUB
    style TA1 text-align:left
    style INT1 text-align:left
    style ISS1 text-align:left
    style SIG1 text-align:left
    style TA2 text-align:left
    style QT text-align:left
    style PPC text-align:left
    style SIG2 text-align:left
```

For **non-qualified EAAs**, the applicable Rulebook specifies how the RP obtains the relevant trust anchor.

##### 5.1.4 Identification Attribute Matching and Shared Identifiers (ETSI TS 119 475 Clause 5.1)

Before looking at WRPAC and WRPRC separately, ETSI TS 119 475 clause `5.1` defines how both artifacts remain linkable to the same registered relying party. The crucial rule is **shared identifier continuity**, not byte-for-byte equality of every display field.

| Clause 5.1 linkage rule | RP-facing consequence |
|:------------------------|:----------------------|
| The WRPRC must include identification attributes of the WRP that match the WRPAC. | Wallets and auditors should expect the two artifacts to describe the same relying party, even if they emphasize different fields. |
| Before issuing a WRPRC, the provider must verify that an identifier in the WRPAC matches an identifier included in the WRPRC. | At least one authoritative register identifier is the hard linkage point across both artifacts. |
| If other identification attributes differ, linkage relies solely on the matching identifier. | Display names or auxiliary fields may vary without breaking certificate coherence. |
| If the WRPRC includes multiple identifiers, at least one must correspond to an identifier present in the WRPAC. | Multi-identifier WRPRCs still need a direct bridge back to the access certificate. |

For legal persons, ETSI TS 119 475 maps register attributes into WRPAC and WRPRC as follows:

| Register attribute | WRPAC expression | WRPRC expression | Practical consequence |
|:-------------------|:-----------------|:-----------------|:----------------------|
| `tradeName` | `commonName` | `name` | User-facing/service-facing display name. |
| `supportURI` | Subject Alternative Name URI | `support_uri` | Support and deletion-contact channel travels into both trust layers. |
| `legalName` | `organizationName` | `sub_ln` | Official legal name is split out explicitly in WRPRC. |
| `establishedBylaw` | Not present | Not present | Register-only legal-basis detail; not carried into either certificate. |
| `type` + `identifier` | `organizationIdentifier` | `sub` | This is the primary legal-person linkage attribute across WRPAC and WRPRC. |
| `postalAddress` | Not present | Not present | Address remains register-only in this mapping. |
| `country` | `countryName` | `country` | Country is present in both artifacts. |
| `email` | SAN `rfc822Name` | Not present | Contact email may appear in WRPAC but not in WRPRC. |
| `phone` | SAN telephone `otherName` | Not present | Telephone may appear in WRPAC but not in WRPRC. |
| `infoURI` | Not present | `info_uri` | Informational/public web pages are a WRPRC-side transparency field, not a WRPAC identity field. |

If `tradeName` is absent, the WRPAC `commonName` falls back to the naming rules of the underlying legal-person certificate profile rather than leaving the subject display empty.
